content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 12, 1990

Linda M. Tangren, Chair
NWPC of CALIFORNIA
123 Rainbow Road
Grass Valley, CA 95949

Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-129

Dear Ms. Tangren:

You have requested advice on behalf of the National Women's
Political Caucus of California ("the Caucus") concerning the
campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ On
March 14, 1990, I telephoned you for additional information
concerning the first question asked in your letter of February 12,
1990. You rephrased the question as follows:

QUESTIONS

1. May the Caucus transfer or loan funds from its general
operating account to its sponsored committee's campaign account?

2. Are there any provisions requiring the campaign account
to have a separate set of bylaws?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Caucus may transfer or loan funds from its general
operating account to its sponsored committee's campaign account as long as the amount
being loaned or transferred does not exceed the contribution
limitations imposed by the Act. You may want to contact the
Franchise Tax Board of the Internal Revenue Service to determine
what the tax implications may be in connection with the making of
such loans or transfers.

2. The Act does not contain any provisions which require an
organization or its campaign committee to establish bylaws.

_________________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

~1    Linda M. Tangren
     Page 2


                                    FACTS

          The National Women's Political Caucus of California was
     formed in February 1971. The Caucus has three separate bank
     accounts: an operating expense fund (the "general fund"), an
     education fund, and a fund for making contributions to candidates
     (the "campaign account"). During our telephone conversation of
     March 14, you indicated that the general fund is comprised of a
     portion of the membership dues paid to the national association.
     You also indicated that the Caucus has not made contributions or
     loans to its campaign account from the general fund in the past
     and that the campaign account normally receives its money from
     direct fundraising.

                                  ANALYSIS

          The Act imposes limitations on contributions made to
     political committees and broad based political committees within a
     fiscal year (July 1-June 30).2/  "Political committee" and "broad
     based political committee" may receive up to $2,500 per fiscal
     year from a person. (Section 85302.) "Person" is defined as an
     individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture,
     syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, association,
     committee, and labor organization. (Section 85102(b).)
     "Political committee" is a committee of persons which receives
     contributions from two or more persons and acting in concert makes
     contributions to candidates. (Section 85102(c).) "Broad based
     political committee" is a committee of persons which has been in
     existence for more than six months, receives contributions from
     one hundred or more persons, and acting in concert makes
     contributions to five or more candidates. (Section 85102(d).)

          Since there has not been a history of making contributions
     from the general fund, the Caucus is limited to transferring not
     more than $2,500 per fiscal year to the campaign account.
     (Section 85302.) Once the Caucus has established a history of
     using a portion of the membership dues for political purposes, the
     members are deemed to be on notice that a portion of their dues
     may, in the future, be used for political purposes. Thereafter,
     the portion of dues used to make contributions will be considered
     contributions from each member and not the Caucus.3/


_______________________________
     2/ The term "contribution" in the Act includes a loan. (Section
     82015.)

     3/ Generally, the Commission has advised that an organization
     which begins making contributions from funds such as membership
     dues is not "receiving" contributions from its members for one
     calendar year (January 1-December 31). That year's activity
     establishes a "history" of making contributions and will put the
     members on notice that a portion of their dues may be used for
     political purposes.

Linda M. Tangren
Page 3

An organization which uses only a portion of the money it
receives (in the form of membership dues) for making contributions
or expenditures to support or oppose candidates or ballot measures
is required to report as "contributions received" only that
portion of its receipts which is actually spent in connection with
supporting or opposing candidates or measures. In order to ensure
that the contributions received by the campaign account do not
exceed the limitations imposed by the Act, you must combine each
member's direct contributions to the campaign account with the
allocable portion of her dues which are transferred from the
general fund to determine how much the campaign account has
received from each contributor.

Section 84211(f) requires that all contributions received
from a single source aggregating $100 or more during a calendar
year be itemized on the committee's campaign statement (Form 420).
The following example illustrates the method for determining what
portion of each member's dues must be counted as a contribution:
If the Caucus receives a total of $100,000 in membership dues and
transfers $10,000 of those dues to the campaign account, 10
percent of the funds received by the Caucus are "contributions"
within the meaning of the Act. Thus, 10 percent of the total
amount of each member's dues counts toward determining whether
that particular member has made a contribution of $100 or more to
the campaign account. If the allocable portion of a member's
dues, when combined with his or her direct contributions to the
campaign account, total $100 or more during a calendar year, that
member's name, address, occupation and employer must be itemized
on the recipient committee campaign statement (Form 420). In
addition, each member may contribute to the campaign account no
more than $2,500 per fiscal year in the form of direct
contributions and dues transferred from the general funds.
(Section 85302.)

We are unable to answer your question concerning the legal
requirements for establishing a separate set of corporate bylaws
for the campaign account. The Act does not address this issue.

I hope the information provided above is helpful. Please
contact me at 916/322-5662 if you have additional questions
concerning this matter.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

BY: Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant
